words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian авария (avarija), itself from Italian avaria, ultimately from Arabic عَوَارِيَّة (ʕawāriyya).

words.etymology: Related to ალაგი (alagi), see there for more.
From Old Georgian ჰალალი (halali), from Arabic حَلَال (ḥalāl). The loss of ჰ (h) is frequent.
Adjective derived from the verb ალაჯებს (alaǯebs). Cognate with Mingrelian ალაჯუნა (alaǯuna), აჲაჯუნა (ayaǯuna).

words.etymology: From Old Georgian ამბოხი (amboxi), of the same Iranian origin as Old Armenian ամբոխ (ambox).

words.etymology: From ამ (am, “this”) + რიგი (rigi, “row, line”) + -ად (-ad, “adverb-forming suffix”). For a similar formation in English, cf. along these lines.

glosses: uploader (someone who or something that uploads files)

words.en: anti-Western person or another kind of actor, someone or something opposing the West
glosses: anti-Western person or another kind of actor, someone or something opposing the West

examples.ka: ანტიდასავლელი აქტორები ძალისხმევას არ იშურებენ, პროპაგანდის მეშვეობით ტვინი აუმღვრიონ მოქალაქეებს
examples.roman: anṭidasavleli akṭorebi ʒalisxmevas ar išureben, ṗroṗagandis mešveobit ṭvini aumɣvrion mokalakeebs
examples.en: Anti-western actors are going all out to brainwash citizens through the means of propaganda

examples.ka: ბოლო ხანებში საქართველოში ანტიდასავლურმა პროპაგანდამ იმატა
examples.roman: bolo xanebši sakartveloši anṭidasavlurma ṗroṗagandam imaṭa
examples.en: Lately, antiwestern propaganda has been on the rise in Georgia

glosses: apparatus, machine (the group that controls a political or similar organization)

words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian апперкот (apperkot), itself from English uppercut.
Borrowed from Russian ажиота́ж (ažiotáž), itself from French agiotage.
From არა- (ara-, “non-,un-”) + ამქვეყნიური (amkveq̇niuri, “earthly, material, of this world”).
From Middle Georgian არავი (aravi), from Old Armenian հարաւ (haraw).

words.etymology: From ასო (aso) + მთავრული (mtavruli, “important; capital, uppercase”), a reference to the fact that the letters of this script were used as capital letters together with later Georgian scripts: Nuskhuri and Mkhedruli.

glosses: place where goods are very expensive
words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian апте́ка (aptéka) undergoing Georgianization to make the word pronounced more freely.

glosses: accent (distinctive pronunciation associated with a region, social group, etc.)

words.etymology: From Old Georgian აღდგომაჲ (aɣdgomay). The term originally meant 'to get up', however, the sense Easter developed from the fact, that "Christ got up (i.e. resurrected)". By surface analysis, აღ- (aɣ-) + დგომა (dgoma).

glosses: agog, anxious, haywire, nervous, alarmed, bothered, flurried, flustered, thrilled

words.etymology: Past participle of the verb აღემატება (aɣemaṭeba).

From აღმოსავალი (aɣmosavali) + -ეთი (-eti), literally “a place (from) where (it) comes up towards us”, which is a metaphor for sun movement, and the fact that it comes from the east. Ultimately from სვლა (svla).

glosses: the act of picking up (animate beings)

examples.en: Don't forget it from now on.

words.etymology: Inherited from Old Georgian აწ (ac̣); see there for more. Doublet of აწ (ac̣).

glosses: right away, right now, now, this instant

words.etymology: From Old Georgian ბადაგი (badagi), an Iranian borrowing; see Persian بَادَه (bāda /bâde, “wine, must”) for more.

words.etymology: From late Middle Georgian ბაკლა (baḳla), probably from Middle Armenian բակլայ (baklay).

words.etymology: Ultimately from Latin barbarismus likely via Russian варвари́зм (varvarízm).

words.etymology: Borrowed from Armenian բաց (bacʻ). Initially probably a Tbilisi dialect word.

words.etymology: Probably borrowed from Russian бинт (bint), from German Binde.

Perhaps from Old Georgian ბიჭი (bič̣i, “crumbs”). For the sense development compare Russian кро́шка (króška, “crumb; baby, little one”).
Alternatively, together with Middle Armenian բիճ (bič, “bastard”) borrowed from Iranian: compare Northern Kurdish bîj, bîjî, Central Kurdish بیژوو (bîjû, “bastard”).
From Old Georgian ბორიაჲ (boriay, “northern wind”), from Ancient Greek Βορέᾱς (Boréās).

glosses: unorganized ill-intentioned large group of people

words.en: insurrection, uprising, riot especially in prison
glosses: insurrection, uprising, riot especially in prison
words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian бунт (bunt), from German Bund (“alliance”).
From Proto-Georgian-Zan *burdɣa- (“down, plumage”). Cognate with Mingrelian ბურდღა (burdɣa), Laz ბუნდღა (bundğa).
From Old Georgian ბურთჳ (burtwi, “ball, sphere, orb”).
From Old Georgian ბურტყლი (burṭq̇li), from Proto-Georgian-Zan *burṭq̇l- (“plumage, down”). Cognate with Mingrelian ბუტყუ (buṭq̇u), Laz ბუტყუ (buťqu), ბურტყუ (burťqu).
